english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is a claim about what it means for a person to have a low ego about what they know. It says that a low‑ego person must have three things: first, the person must have a true look at how far their own know and brain skill can go; second, the person must not say they know more than their skill really lets them; and third, the person must be ready to say when they are wrong and to give credit when other people add something useful.

The second idea is a different case that is meant to show that the three things do not always go together. In this case a person can have a true look at the edge of their own know and brain skill, and can also not say they know more than their skill lets them, but still not be ready to say when they are wrong or to give credit to others. Yet other people still see this person as having a low ego about what they know because the person often listens and does not act like they are the only source of good ideas. This different case is used to say that the three‑thing description does not capture all the ways a low‑ego person can act, so the “if and only if” link in the first idea is wrong.

The third idea looks at whether the thinking in the case is good. The reasoning that a single different case can break a definition that uses “if and only if” is normally a strong move, because a single counter‑example does show that a definition that says “all and only those who have the three things are low‑ego” cannot be true. The thinking is good if the different case really does show a person who is low‑ego while missing the third thing. If the case does not truly show a low‑ego person, or if people would not call that person low‑ego without the third thing, then the reasoning would be weaker. Overall, the structure of the argument – stating a definition, giving a case that does not fit the definition but seems to fit the idea, and then saying the definition must be wrong – is a solid way to test a definition, so the thinking behind the case is generally good.

english_query: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a way to tell when a statement belongs to a special kind of knowledge. The claim is that such a statement must have three parts. First, the part we say about the thing must not already be inside the idea of the thing itself. Second, we must be able to know the statement without any need to see or feel anything in the world. Third, the rightness of the statement must come from the simple ways our mind can see or sort things, not from any particular thing we have seen.  

The second idea is an example that the speaker says shows the three parts do not always line up the way the first idea says. They point to a statement that looks like it meets the three parts, but they argue that it is not really of the special kind the first idea talks about. For instance, they might talk about a statement about numbers that seems to be known without seeing any objects and whose saying part is not already in the idea of the numbers, yet they say its rightness comes from the way we count, which they think is not one of the simple ways the mind can see. Because the statement does not fit the label the first idea gives, they say it is a counter‑example.  

From this they think it follows that the three‑part rule cannot be a perfect way to pick out that special kind of knowledge. If one statement can meet the three parts and still not be of that kind, then the rule must be wrong or at least incomplete. So the conclusion they draw is that the original definition needs to be changed or dropped.  

Looking at the thinking, the move from the example to the conclusion is only as good as the claim that the example really does meet all three parts while still not being of the special kind. If the example actually slips on one of the three parts, or if the idea of “simple ways our mind can see” is understood differently, then the conclusion does not follow. The reasoning is clear in that it shows a possible mismatch, but the strength of the point depends on whether the example truly satisfies the three conditions as they are meant. If the example does satisfy them, the thinking is strong and shows a real problem with the rule. If the example does not satisfy them, the thinking is weak because it would be based on a mistaken claim about the example. In short, the argument is only good if the example really is what the speaker says it is.

english_query: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward says that cutting up the story of the past into parts can be called right only when three things are true. The first thing is that each part has a set of things that are the same for the things that happen inside that part. The second thing is that the lines that separate the parts line up with big changes or breaks in the way things happen. The third thing is that the way we cut up the past works for the whole area that we are looking at, not just for one small place.

The second idea gives an example that they say shows the opposite. They talk about a part of the past that they call the “Industrial time” in Europe. They say that this part has the same set of things – new machines, factories, and city growth – for all the things that happen inside it. They also say that the line that starts this part is the moment when the first big factories were built, which is a big break from the way things were before. Finally they say that this way of cutting up the past works for the whole area of Europe because most of the countries there saw the same new machines and factories. Yet they point out that in many other places, such as large parts of Asia, the same “Industrial time” does not show the same set of things and the line does not line up with a big break there. So they claim that even though the three things seem to be met in Europe, the way of cutting up the past still does not work for the whole area that was meant to be covered.

The third idea that follows from this example is that the claim with the three things cannot be true in general. Because they have found one case that meets the three things in one place but does not work for other places, they say the whole rule that the three things are enough must be wrong.

The fourth idea looks at whether the thinking is good. The logic that one example that goes against the rule shows the rule is false is a correct kind of thinking – a single opposite case can break a rule that says “all cases must have these three things”. However, the strength of the argument depends on whether the example truly has all three things as described. If the “Industrial time” in Europe does not really have the same set of things for every country, or if the line does not truly match a big break for all of Europe, then the example does not fully meet the three conditions and the argument is weaker. If the example does meet the three conditions in Europe but still fails for other places, then the reasoning is strong and shows that the rule is not enough to make a good cut‑up of the past for the whole world. In that case the thinking is good because it points out that a rule that works in one area does not automatically work everywhere.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim about what makes a statement always true. It says that a statement is always true exactly when three things happen: the statement is true, saying that the statement is not true creates a problem that cannot be true, and we can know that the statement is true without using what we see. The claim puts these three parts together and says that they are both needed and enough for a statement to be always true.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the claim is wrong. The example picks a statement that is true, but where saying it is not does not lead to a problem that cannot be true, and where we cannot know it without using what we see. For instance, a statement about what the weather is right now can be true, but saying it is not does not cause a logical problem, and we can only know if it is true by looking at the sky. Because this statement meets the first part of the claim but not the other two, yet it is not an always‑true statement, the example is meant to show that the three parts do not guarantee an always‑true statement.

The thinking that follows from the two ideas is that the original claim about always‑true statements is not reliable. The reasoning is good in the sense that it points out a clear mismatch: a statement can be true without fitting the other two conditions, so the three conditions cannot be the exact test for always‑true statements. However, the evaluation also depends on how we understand “a problem that cannot be true” and “knowing without using what we see.” If those phrases are taken in a stricter way, the example might not break the claim. Still, the overall move – to find a case where the three parts do not line up with an always‑true statement – is a solid way to show the original claim has a flaw.

english_query: counterexample to: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is that there is a place that has a certain kind of weather even though it does not meet any of the three things that were said to be needed for that kind of weather. They say that the place’s average heat and rain do not stay the same for a long time, that the place cannot be put into any known weather group, and that the place’s weather is not set by its north‑south position or its height above sea level. The second idea they draw from this is that because such a place exists, the statement that a place has a particular kind of weather only when all three of those things are true must be wrong. In other words, they think the existence of the place shows that the “if and only if” claim does not hold. The third idea that follows in their mind is that we should not use those three things as the only way to decide what kind of weather a place has.

Looking at the thinking, the move from the first idea to the second is a common way to test a claim: if you can find a case where the claim says something should be true but it is not, then the claim is false. That part of the reasoning is sound, as long as the place really does have the kind of weather they say it has. If the place’s weather does not actually fit the description, then the example does not break the claim and the reasoning falls apart. The step from the second idea to the third idea is also logical: once a claim is shown to be false, it cannot be used as a rule. However, the overall quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly has the weather in question while lacking the three listed features. If the example meets at least one of the three features, or if its weather is not really the kind being talked about, then the reasoning is weak. If the example does meet all the conditions of the counter‑case, then the thinking is good and the original “if and only if” statement should be changed.

english_query: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward is a claim that there is a set of numbers that has three particular features. The first feature is that for any element in the set you can find a counting number that is bigger than it. The second feature is that the set includes every fraction made from two whole numbers, and it even includes all the fractions that we normally call rational numbers. The third feature is that every element of the set can be written as one whole number divided by another whole number. The person says that this set also has the property that there are no missing points – that is, it is full in the sense that every point that should be there is there.

From these three features, the second idea the person draws is that the usual statement “a set of numbers is full if and only if it has those three features” must be wrong. In other words, because this particular set has all three features but still is not full, the “if and only if” claim cannot be true. The person is using the example as a way to show that the rule does not work in every case.

To see whether the thinking is good, we look at the link between the three features and the idea of being full. The reasoning works only if the example truly has all three features and still is not full. If the example does indeed have a counting number larger than every element, contains all fractions, and lets every element be written as one whole number over another, then it does break the “if and only if” claim and the thinking is correct. However, if any part of the example fails – for instance, if the set does not really contain every fraction or if it actually is full when examined more closely – then the conclusion does not follow and the reasoning would be faulty. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example really meets all three stated conditions while lacking the full‑ness property. If it does, the argument is solid; if it does not, the argument falls apart.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward says that a word has meaning only when three things happen. First, the word must be tied to a picture in the mind of the person who says it. Second, that person must be able to use the word to talk with other people. Third, the word must be part of a normal spoken language that a group of people use. The second idea they give is an example that they say shows the three things are not all needed. They tell about a sound that a small child makes to ask for milk. The child has a clear picture of milk in the mind, can use the sound to tell the mother what is wanted, but the sound is not a word that belongs to any normal spoken language used by a group. They say that because this sound still works as a word for the child, the three‑thing rule does not hold. The third idea is their judgment that the rule is therefore false. They think that finding even one case where a word works without meeting one of the three points is enough to knock the whole rule down.

Looking at what they say is true, the example of the child’s special sound is indeed a real situation where a “word” can have meaning without being part of a larger spoken group. The picture in the child’s mind and the ability to use the sound to talk with the mother are both there, while the third point about belonging to a group language is missing. So the factual part of their case is correct. 

What they think follows from that fact is that the three‑thing rule cannot be right. This step follows the normal way we check a rule that says “all cases must have these three parts.” If we can point to even one case that does not have all three parts but still works, the rule cannot be true for every case. That line of thinking is a good one, because a single counter‑example is enough to show a universal claim is false. 

Therefore, the breakdown is: the first idea is the rule itself, the second idea is the real example that breaks the rule, and the third idea is the conclusion that the rule is false. The example they give is true, the jump from the example to the conclusion is a correct move, and the overall thinking is solid.

english_query: counterexample to: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that there is a place we call Y that meets three simple facts. The first fact is that Y cannot be split into two parts that both have at least one point, that both look open, and that do not share any point. The second fact is that any two points in Y can be linked by a line that does not break and that stays completely inside Y. The third fact is that any rule that takes each point in Y and gives a whole number gives the same whole number for every point. They say all three of these facts are true for Y.

The second idea they draw from those facts is that, because the three facts are true, Y should be a single whole piece, that is, it should not be able to be broken into separate pieces. They then say that Y is in fact not a single whole piece, and so the original statement that “a place is a single whole piece if and only if the three facts hold” must be wrong. In other words, they think the three facts do not guarantee that the place is a single whole piece, and their Y shows this.

The third idea is about how good that line of thought is. If Y really does have the three facts and yet is not a single whole piece, then the original statement is indeed false, and the thinking that a single case can show a universal claim wrong is correct. But if Y fails any one of the three facts – for example if it can actually be split into two open parts, or if some two points cannot be linked by an unbroken line inside Y, or if a rule can give different whole numbers at different points – then the claim that the three facts lead to a single whole piece would not be broken, and the reasoning would be faulty. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether Y truly satisfies all three listed facts while still being more than one piece. If it does, the reasoning is good; if it does not, the reasoning is not good.

english_query: counterexample to: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively strong if and only if (i) the observed instances are numerous, (ii) the observed instances are varied in their circumstances, and (iii) no observed instance contradicts the general conclusion.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that they have a case where a lot of small things are seen, the small things happen in many different ways, and none of those small things go against the big idea they are talking about. They say that all three of these points are true for their case, so the description of the case fits the three points that were given.  

From this they move to the second idea, which is that because the case meets all three points but the big idea still does not hold for all, the original claim that the three points are enough to make a good guess about all must be wrong. In other words they think the original claim is not always right, and that their case shows a situation where the three points are present yet the big idea fails.  

The third idea is an evaluation of the thinking. Since they have shown a case that truly has many small things, many different ways, and no small thing that goes against the big idea, and yet the big idea does not work for everything, the step from the case to the claim being wrong is a good step. The reasoning follows the rule that one single case that meets the stated conditions and still breaks the claim is enough to show the claim cannot be true in all situations. So the thinking that the original claim is not always right is a sound piece of thinking.

english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is true is that a story that tries to explain why a good all‑powerful god lets bad things happen can only work when three things are in place. The first thing is that the story must give an enough why for the god’s permission of bad things. The second thing is that the why must fit with the god being all‑powerful and good. The third thing is that the story must explain the amount of bad things we see and where those bad things are in the world.

From that they think that if we can find a case where a story meets those three things but still does not make the idea work, then the claim that those three things are enough is wrong. In other words they think the example shows that the three things are not enough to make a story work, so the original claim fails.

Whether the thinking is good depends on how well the example really meets the three things and still fails. If the example truly gives an enough why, fits with a god who is all‑powerful and good, and explains the amount and where bad things are, and yet still does not make the idea work, then the example does show that the original claim is not always true. On the other hand, if the example slips on any of the three parts – for instance if the why is not really enough, or if the why does not really fit with a god who is all‑powerful and good, or if it does not fully explain the amount and where bad things are – then the reasoning is weak because it does not actually break the claim. So the quality of the thinking rests on how accurately the example follows the three conditions and whether the failure of the story is clear. If those points are solid, the thinking is strong; if not, the thinking is not very good.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person is talking about is a claim that a talk is right if and only if three things happen. The first thing is that the talk has a group of start points and an end point. The second thing is that the end point can be reached from the start points by using the ways that a known set of ways says we can move from one point to another. The third thing is that every move in the chain is allowed by a clearly said way to move.

The second idea is an example that the person says does not fit the claim. In this example there is a talk that has a start point and an end point and the end point does seem to follow from the start points, but the chain of moves uses a way that is not part of the known set of ways. The person points out that even though the chain looks like it follows the start points, the missing or different way means the claim’s three things are not all true for this talk. Because the chain still gets to the end point, the person says the claim that all three things are needed for a talk to be right is broken.

The third idea is the judgment about the thinking. The person thinks that because they have found one talk that reaches an end point without using only the known ways, the claim that a talk is right only when the three things happen must be wrong. This thinking is good only if the example really does show a talk that reaches an end point while still being a talk at all. If the example uses a move that is not allowed by the known ways, then the example is not a proper talk under the same rules the claim is talking about, so the example does not really break the claim. If, however, the example is still a talk that follows the same idea of a talk, then the claim does indeed have a problem. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example is truly a talk that belongs to the same group that the claim talks about. If it is, the thinking is strong; if it is not, the thinking is weak.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a claim about what makes a statement always true. It says that a statement is always true when three things happen. First, the statement must be true in every possible case that could happen. Second, we must be able to see that the statement is true just by looking at the shape of the words, without needing to know whether any of the smaller parts are true or false. Third, the statement must be built only with the usual words that join other statements, such as “and”, “or”, and “not”.

The second idea is the claim that those three things together are enough to label a statement as always true, and that nothing else is needed. In other words, the person thinks that if a statement meets all three of the points above, then we can safely say it is an always‑true statement, and that any always‑true statement must meet those three points.

The thinking is tested by looking at a situation that shows the claim does not always hold. Consider the statement “All cats are cats.” This statement is true in every possible case, because any thing that is a cat is also a cat. We can also see that it is true just by its shape: it repeats the same word on both sides, so no matter what the word means, the statement cannot be false. However, the statement uses the word “all,” which is not one of the usual joining words like “and,” “or,” or “not.” Because the third point is broken, the statement still satisfies the first two points but is still an always‑true statement. This shows that the three points are not a perfect match for the idea of an always‑true statement.

Because a situation like “All cats are cats” exists, the reasoning that the three points are exactly what makes a statement always true is not good. The first idea about the three points is partly true, but the jump to the second idea that those points are both necessary and sufficient is false. The counter‑situation shows that a statement can be always true even when it does not use only the usual joining words, so the overall argument does not hold.

english_query: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
ugf_response: First, the person who makes the case says that there is a thing that can be named with a short sentence that is true for just the things that have that way, and that the way only looks at what the things are, not at how we talk about them. Then they think that because this case shows a break in the idea that a short sentence can name a way only when the way only looks at what things are, the whole saying must be wrong. Finally, we can look at whether the step from the case to the saying being wrong is a good step. If the case really does meet every one of the things they say it does, then the step is good because it shows a real break in the idea. If, however, the case does not really meet every one of the things, for example if the short sentence is not really short or if the way does depend on how we talk, then the step is not good because it would be based on something wrong.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a person has broken a promise when three things happen: the person said words that they would do a certain thing, another person trusted those words, and the first person did not do the thing. The case that is being talked about says that all three of those things really did happen. It tells us that one person spoke about doing something, that another person trusted that speech, and that the first person then did not do the thing. So the description of what happened in the case is true according to the first idea.

From that, the second idea says that because we can find a real case where the three things happen, the first idea must be wrong. It thinks that the first idea cannot be right, because the first idea claimed that those three things are exactly what makes a promise broken, and the case shows a situation that fits the three things but, in the view of the speaker, does not count as a broken promise. Therefore the second idea follows that the first idea does not work as a full rule.

To see whether that thinking is good, we have to check if the case really meets each of the three points in the way the first idea meant them. If the words spoken were not clear enough about the thing to be done, or if the trust was not reasonable, then the case might not actually fit the three points as the first idea meant them. In that situation the thinking would be weak because it would be using a case that does not truly match the rule. If, however, the words were clear, the trust was reasonable, and the action was truly not done, then the case does match the three points and shows that the first idea does not cover all situations. In that case the thinking that the first idea is wrong would be good. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the case really satisfies each of the three parts in the way the first idea intended.

english_query: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is that there is an example where two groups have the same amount of things, but you cannot find a rule that takes each thing in the first group to a different thing in the second group and also makes sure that every thing in the second group gets a match. In other words, they claim they have a case where the two groups are the same size but the rule that is supposed to work for all same‑size groups does not work here.

The second idea they take from that example is that because they think the rule fails in this case, the rule itself must be wrong. They think that the statement “two groups have the same size if and only if there is a rule that matches each thing in the first group to a different thing in the second group and covers every thing in the second group” is therefore not true for all groups.

When we look at the first idea, we see that it is not true. The rule that each thing in one group can be matched to a different thing in the other group and that every thing in the second group gets a match is actually a correct way to tell if two groups have the same amount. Any time two groups have the same amount, you can always build such a rule, and any time such a rule exists, the two groups must have the same amount. The example that was given does not really break the rule; it either missed a way to make the matches or described the groups incorrectly. Because the first idea is false, the thinking that the rule is wrong is not good. The conclusion they draw does not follow, since it rests on a claim that does not hold. So the overall argument is not a good one.

english_query: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is true is a claim about what it means for a person to be not too much in any feeling or act. They say that a person is not too much in any feeling or act if and only if three things hold. The first thing is that the person keeps their strong wants in the middle rather than letting them run wild. The second thing is that the person does this because they feel calm inside, not because they try for a short time and push themselves. The third thing is that the person feels no fight inside when they keep their strong wants in the middle. They present these three points as the whole truth about being not too much.

The second idea they think follows is that any person who meets all three of those points must be not too much in any feeling or act. To try to show that this is not always true, they give a case that shows the rule does not work. In this case there is a person who does keep their strong wants in the middle, who does it because they feel calm inside, and who feels no fight inside while doing it, but most people would still not call that person not too much in any feeling or act because that person often acts in ways that are too much in other parts of life, such as being angry or greedy in other moments. The case is meant to show that meeting the three points does not guarantee the whole idea of being not too much.

The third idea is an assessment of the thinking. Because the case shows a person can have the three things and still not be not too much, the thought that the three things are enough to define the whole idea is not good. The reasoning leaves out other parts of how a person shows a balanced way of feeling and acting. Therefore the argument that the three points fully describe a not‑too‑much person is weak, and the thinking needs more than just those three points to be reliable.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
ugf_response: The first idea they say is true is that they have an example of a talk that starts with things we can know just by thinking, that uses only steps that are sure to keep truth, and that ends by saying a god that has all good things is there. They tell us that this talk meets the first two parts of the rule about a good talk for a god, but it does not meet the third part because the end point does not say that a god with all good things exists. The second idea they think follows is that because they can make a talk that has the first two parts and still does not get the end point about a god with all good things, the rule that says a talk is good exactly when it has all three parts must be wrong. In other words, they think the rule does not work as a test for a good talk about a god. The third idea is an evaluation of the thinking. If the example really does have only things we can know by thinking and only sure steps, then it does show that having those two parts is not enough to guarantee the end point about a god with all good things, so the thinking that the rule is false is good. If, however, the example slips on either the starting things or the sure steps, then the example does not really break the rule and the claim that the rule is false would be weak. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly satisfies the first two parts; if it does, the reasoning is solid, and if it does not, the reasoning is not convincing.

english_query: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
ugf_response:
The first idea is the rule that a story about the past is called a good past story only when three things are true. The first of those things is that the story tells the things that happened in the right order of time. The second thing is that the story is built on real first places we get info from about the past. The third thing is that the story gives a clear explanation of why the things happened in the way they did and that explanation makes sense.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the rule does not work. In this example a story does tell the things in the right order, it does use real first places we get info from, and it does give a clear explanation that makes sense. Yet the person who gives the example says that most people still do not think the story is a good past story because the story leaves out a big part of how people felt or because the story pushes a certain view that many think is unfair. In this way the example is meant to show that even when the three things are met, the story can still be seen as not a good past story.

The third idea is the claim that because this example exists, the rule cannot be true. The reasoning goes: if a rule says that three things are enough to make a story a good past story, and we have a story that has those three things but still is not seen as a good past story, then the rule must be wrong. So the person thinks the rule is disproved by the example.

The fourth idea is an evaluation of that reasoning. To see if the thinking is good we need to check whether the example really does have all three things the rule talks about. If the story leaves out something that the rule counts as part of the real first places we get info from, or if the explanation does not really make sense to most people, then the example does not truly meet the three things and the rule is not yet disproved. If, however, the story truly does meet the three things and still is judged bad for other reasons, then the rule may indeed be missing something important, and the reasoning that the rule is false would be strong. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the example fits the three conditions and on whether the rule was meant to cover the extra part that the example brings up. If the example is accurate, the thinking is good; if the example is not accurate, the thinking is weak.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a claim about what it means for a person to have a belief that is “ready to act.” The claim says that a belief is of that kind only when three things happen. First, the person must be ready to say yes to the idea when the question is asked in the right way. Second, that readiness must come from some earlier thought or some earlier way the person’s mind worked. Third, the person would, under the usual things that happen, say yes to the idea truly when they think about it in a calm way.

The second idea is an example that is meant to show that the three things do not always line up the way the claim says. Imagine a person who, when asked a certain question, says yes because they want to fit in with others, not because they truly think the idea is right. The person’s yes‑answer is ready when the question is asked, so the first part of the claim is met. The person’s readiness comes from a memory of a time they were praised for saying yes, so the second part of the claim is also met. But when the person sits alone later and thinks about the idea, they do not truly believe it and would not say yes if they were alone. In that quiet thinking the person would not say yes sincerely, so the third part of the claim is not met. Yet the person still acted as if they had the ready belief when the question was asked. This shows that the three parts of the claim do not always work together to pick out the kind of belief the claim wants to talk about.

The third idea is the judgment that the reasoning used to give the example is good. The reasoning first takes the three parts of the claim and then shows a situation where the first two parts are true while the third part is false, yet the person still behaves as the claim says a ready belief would. Because the example fits the claim’s own description of the first two parts and still leads to a different result, it shows that the claim does not capture all the ways a person can be ready to say yes. In other words, the reasoning correctly points out a gap in the original claim, and the example works as a clear illustration of that gap. Therefore the thinking that the claim is not correct because of this example is a good piece of thinking.

english_query: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that the statement says a rule that takes a number and gives another number does not have a jump at a certain point exactly when three things are true: the rule gives a result for that point, every list of numbers that gets closer and closer to that point makes the list of results get closer and closer to the result at the point, and the rule changes any group of numbers that stay inside some size into another group of numbers that also stay inside some size, no matter how small an area around the point we look at. The second idea is that the person shows a rule that follows the first two things but does not follow the third thing, yet the rule still has no jump at the point. In other words, they give a rule that for any list of numbers that gets closer to the point, the results also get closer, and the rule does give a result at the point, but there is a group of numbers that stays inside a size near the point whose results do not stay inside any size. This shows that the third condition is not needed for the rule to have no jump at the point, so the original statement is not true.

The third idea the person takes from this is that the original “if and only if” claim is false because they have found a rule that meets the first two requirements and still has no jump, while breaking the third requirement. They think that because this one rule works, the whole claim must be wrong.

The fourth idea is to judge whether this line of thinking is good. The example they give does indeed meet the first two requirements and still has no jump, so it correctly shows that the third requirement is not necessary. That means the claim that all three things are needed is false. The reasoning that a single example that breaks one part of the claim is enough to show the whole claim is false is sound, because “if and only if” means both ways must hold for every rule. Finding one rule where the third part fails while the other parts hold is enough to prove the whole statement does not work. So the thinking is good and the conclusion follows correctly from the example.

english_query: counterexample to: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
ugf_response:
The first thought is about a statement that says a person’s seeing or feeling of things leads to their doing only when three things are true. The three things are: first, the person must be aware of the thing they see or feel that matters for what they are about to do; second, what they see or feel must make them pick a certain move; third, how well the move works must rely on how true the seeing or feeling was.

The second thought gives a different example that is meant to show the statement can be wrong. In this example a person moves a lever after a flash of light, but the person does not actually notice the flash; the move happens because the light triggers a hidden part of the body that works without the person’s awareness. The move still works well, even though the person was not aware of the flash and the flash did not decide the move. The facts that the person did not notice the flash and that the move was still correct are the parts that the example says are true.

The third thought is the claim that because the facts in the example are true, the original three‑part statement must be false. The reasoning goes: if a move can happen correctly without the person being aware of the thing they see or feel, then the first part of the three‑part rule is not needed; if the move can happen without the thing they see or feel deciding it, then the second part is not needed; and if the move can be correct even when the thing they see or feel is not true, then the third part is not needed. Therefore the whole rule does not hold.

The fourth thought looks at whether that reasoning is good. The step from the facts to the claim that the whole rule is false is shaky because the example only shows that one of the three parts can be missing in one special case. It does not prove that the other two parts are never needed, nor that the rule cannot work in other situations. A better view might be that the rule works for many cases but that there are rare cases where a move can happen without all three parts. So the thinking that the example completely knocks out the rule is not fully convincing, even though the facts of the example are clear.

english_query: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a description of a whole number that they call “special.” They say a whole number is special if three things are true: first, the number is bigger than one; second, you cannot write the number as a result of putting together two whole numbers that are each bigger than one; third, if you take one away from the number, the result cannot be split into equal groups that each have the size of the original number. They present these three points as true statements about what makes a number special.

From those three points they think they can decide whether a number is special or not. They say that if a number follows all three points, then it must be special, and if they can find a number that follows the three points but is not special, that would show the description is wrong. To try this, they look at the number two. Two is bigger than one, you cannot put together two whole numbers each bigger than one to get two, and if you take one away you get one, which cannot be split into equal groups that each have size two. Because the three points are true for two, they say the rule says two should be special, but they claim two is not special. They then say this mismatch is a case that shows the rule does not work.

The thinking has a problem. The first two points are indeed the usual way to tell if a whole number is special, but the third point does not add any new information: for any whole number bigger than one, taking one away will always give a result that cannot be split into equal groups of the original size. So the third point is true for every such number and does not help to separate special numbers from non‑special ones. Moreover, the claim that two is not special is false; by the usual meaning, two does satisfy the first two points and is therefore special. Because the claim about two is wrong, the case they present does not actually break the description. In short, the first idea is partly true, the second idea tries to use a false claim to prove the description wrong, and the reasoning does not hold up.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
ugf_response: The first idea that the person making the case says is true is that there is a situation where one person has been using a piece of land for a long stretch of time, has never stopped using it, has used it in a way that anyone can see, and has used it without asking the person who really has the right to own that land. The case says that even with all of those things the person who has been using the land does not get the right to own it. The second idea that follows from that fact is that the rule that says “if a person has used land for a long time, openly, and without the real owner’s permission, then that person gets the right to own the land” must be wrong, because the example shows a case where the three things happen but the right to own does not appear. The third idea is an evaluation of whether that thinking is good. To decide if the thinking is good we have to check whether the example truly meets all three parts of the rule. If the example really does have the long use, the open use, and the use without permission, then the example does show that the rule is not enough by itself and the thinking that the rule is false is strong. But if the example is missing even one of those parts—for instance if the use was not truly open to everyone, or if the real owner had given some hidden permission, or if the use was not truly without stopping—then the example does not break the rule and the thinking that the rule is false would be weak. So the quality of the reasoning depends on whether the example really fits all three conditions; if it does, the reasoning is good, and if it does not, the reasoning is not convincing.

english_query: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward says that a whole thing is made of a group of pieces when three things are true. First, each piece must be inside the edges of the whole. Second, the pieces together must fill every part of the whole so that there is no place inside the whole that is left empty. Third, the pieces must not share the same place inside the whole; no spot can belong to more than one piece at the same time. The claim says that these three points are both needed and enough for something to be called a whole made of parts.

The second idea gives an example that is meant to show that the three points are not enough. In this example there is a thing that looks like a whole and is made of several pieces that are each inside its edges, that together fill all the space inside, and that do not share any spot. Yet, when we look at the thing we see that it does not behave like a normal whole made of parts – for instance it may act as a single piece itself or it may have a property that the three points do not explain. Because this example seems to follow the three points but still does not fit the idea of a whole made of parts, the example is used to argue that the three points cannot be the whole story.

Looking at the thinking behind the example, the step from the first idea to the second is a move that tries to show the rule is wrong by finding a case that meets the rule but seems to break the meaning of a whole. This move is a common way to test a rule, and it works well when the example really does meet all three points. If the example does meet the three points, then the reasoning that the rule is not enough is strong. However, if the example actually fails one of the three points in a way that is not obvious – for example if two pieces do share a tiny spot or if a tiny part of the whole is not covered – then the move is not strong because the example would not truly follow the rule. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly satisfies the three conditions. If it does, the thinking is good and shows the rule needs more detail. If it does not, the thinking is weak because it attacks a rule with a case that does not really fit the rule.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is true is a claim about when a substance can be called a basic part of matter. They say this is true only when three things are true. First, all the tiny pieces that make the substance up have the same number that tells how many inside each piece. Second, the substance has a set of ways it acts that are different from all other basic parts. Third, the substance is all the same throughout when we look at it in usual places.

The second idea they think follows is that there is a case that meets the first two things but not the third, and that this case shows the claim is not always right. They point to a substance that is made of tiny pieces with the same number, but that can exist in more than one look at the same time, for example both a hard form and an air form, in usual places. Because it can be in two different looks, it does not stay all the same throughout, even though it meets the first two points. This is used as a case that breaks the claim.

The third idea is an assessment of the thinking. The first idea is a clear statement of what they think a basic part is. The second idea gives a real case that meets the first two points but not the third. If the case is right, then the thinking that all three points must hold is not good. So the reasoning that the case shows the claim is wrong is good, as long as the case really does have the features they say. If the case were wrong, then the reasoning would fail. So the argument works if the facts about the case are right.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is that there is a thing we call a basic part of matter that cannot be broken down by any chemical way, that is made of only one kind of tiny center bit, and that we have not found this thing in nature on our planet. They point to a specific basic part that scientists have made in a lab but that does not appear naturally. The claim that this lab‑made basic part cannot be broken down by chemistry is true, because no chemical process can split a basic part into something simpler. The claim that all of its tiny atoms have the same number of center bits is also true, because that is how we define a basic part. The claim that it does not occur naturally on our planet is true for the newest basic parts that have only been produced by people. So the facts they give about this lab‑made basic part are correct.

The second idea they draw from those facts is that the statement “a thing is a basic part of matter if and only if it meets the three conditions” must be wrong. Because they have found a thing that meets the first two conditions but not the third, yet we still call it a basic part, they say this shows the “only if” side of the statement cannot be right. If a single example can be found that breaks a claim that says “all things that have these three features are the only things that are basic parts,” then the whole claim is false. In that sense the reasoning that one counter‑example is enough to knock down an “if and only if” claim is a good piece of thinking.

The third idea they suggest, without spelling it out, is that we should change the definition to leave out the need for a thing to be found in nature. Whether that step is good depends on whether we want a definition that only talks about the inner make‑up of the thing and not about where we can find it. The reasoning that the example forces a change in the definition is reasonable, because the example does show the old definition does not match what scientists now call a basic part. However, the decision to drop the natural‑occurrence condition also has to think about why that condition was added in the first place – perhaps to keep the word from being used for things that are only made in a lab. So the thinking that the example shows a problem with the old rule is solid, while the judgment about how to fix the rule needs a little more discussion.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
ugf_response:
The first idea the person says is that there is a group of people who, over a certain stretch of time, saw most of its members get better at getting food, shelter and other things they need, saw the range of freedoms that members could enjoy grow, and saw the group become better at making new ideas and passing those ideas on to others. The person says these three things really did happen for this group, and they give details that show the rise in material well‑being, the widening of freedoms, and the increase in making and sharing ideas.

The second idea the person says follows from those facts is that the statement “a group has real forward change over a time only when those three things all happen” must be wrong. Because the person can point to a case where those three things happened but the group still did not have real forward change, they say the original claim cannot be true. In other words, they think the example they gave shows the original rule does not work in all cases.

The third idea is an assessment of whether the thinking is good. The reasoning is a kind of test where one example that does not fit the rule is used to say the rule is false. This works if the example is correct and if the three things really did happen as described. It also works if the idea of “real forward change” is understood the same way in the example as in the rule. If the facts about the example are right and the meaning of “real forward change” is the same, then the thinking is strong because one counter‑example is enough to break a rule that says “only if”. If, however, the example is missing some part of the picture, or if the way “real forward change” is being judged is different, then the thinking may be weak. So the quality of the reasoning depends on how well the facts match the claim and how clear the meaning of the key words is in both places.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
ugf_response:
The first idea says that a talk that tries to show a big being is strong only when three things are true. First, it must start from facts that most people agree on. Second, the step that says the big being is the only good reason for those facts must be the only good reason we can think of. Third, the end of the talk must say what the big being is like.

The second idea in the case is that there is a different case that shows the three things are not needed for a talk to be strong. In that case the talk does not use facts that most people agree on, or it does not make the big being the only good reason, or it does not say what the big being is like, yet people still find the talk strong. Because this different case exists, the person making the case says that the first idea is not true.

The thinking in the second idea is pretty good if the different case really does show a strong talk without all three parts. If the example really works, then it shows the rule in the first idea can be broken, so the rule does not hold for every talk. On the other hand, if the example fails to make people think the talk is strong, then the step from the example to saying the rule is false would be a mistake. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the example actually shows a strong talk without the three parts. If it does, the thinking is solid; if it does not, the thinking is weak.

english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person is saying is that there is a case where a time point has happened, is not happening now, and is kept in the mind of at least one aware person, yet we would not call that time point part of the past. They point to a situation like a story that is being told over and over again, where the event is still alive in the telling, so they claim the event is not really past even though it meets the three facts. The second idea they draw from this is that because this case seems to fit the three facts but does not feel like a past time point, the rule that says a time point is past if and only if those three facts are true must be wrong. In other words, they think the rule does not match what we actually think about time.

To see if the thinking is good, we first check whether the example really does have all three facts. The event did happen, it is not happening now, and at least one aware person remembers it, so the three facts are true. Next we ask whether the feeling that the event is still alive in a story means it is not past. The feeling that something is still being talked about does not change the fact that the event itself is finished and not happening now. It only means that the memory of the event is being used in a new way. So the jump from “the event is still talked about” to “the event is not past” does not follow. The reasoning mixes the state of the event with the way we talk about it, and that mix makes the conclusion weak. Therefore, while the first idea is correct about the three facts, the second idea that the rule is false does not hold up because the example does not actually break the rule; it only shows a different way we think about the event after it has happened. The thinking is therefore not a good proof that the rule is wrong.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward says that a kind of action can be called natural only when three things are true. First, the action is done by the whole group of the kind. Second, the action shows up without any teaching before it happens. Third, the action is done in the same way each time for each one and does not change from one time to another. The person giving the case says they have an example that fits all three of those points but that most people would not call that action natural. They point to a type of animal that always builds a place to live in the same way, does it without ever being shown how, and does it the same way for every animal and every time. Because this example seems to meet the three points, they say the definition that ties natural action to those three points must be wrong or at least incomplete.

The second idea they think follows is that if one example can meet the three points and still not be thought of as natural, then the rule that says “only when the three points are true the action is natural” cannot be correct. From this they conclude that the rule needs to be changed, either by adding more conditions or by removing some of the existing ones, so that the rule matches what we actually think of as natural actions.

The thinking behind this argument can be looked at in a few ways. It is good that they try to test a rule by looking for a case that might break it, because that is a common way to see if a rule works. However, the strength of the argument depends on whether the example truly meets all three points. If the whole group does not always do the action, or if some teaching or copying happens early in life, then the example does not really match the three points. If the way the action is done can change a little from one animal to another, then the third point is not fully met either. Because those details can be hard to know for sure, the claim that the example definitely breaks the rule may be on shaky ground. So the overall reasoning is useful as a way to question the rule, but it may not be enough by itself to prove that the rule is false unless the example can be shown to satisfy the three points without any doubt.
